content:
I am most interested in how organizations methodize and capitalize knowledge about human behavior.
My current project examines how corporate participation in open science recasts knowledge infrastructure in computer vision, a rapidly commercializing subfield of computer science that mobilizes massive human behavioral and biometric data and has potentially transformative consequences on surveillance and stratification.
To answer this question, my research intersects network analysis, sociology and history of science, organizational theory, economic sociology, cultural sociology, and computational social science. I am also broadly interested in methods for exploratory analysis and causal inference on large-scale text and network data.
title: Research

content:
In addition to teaching in my substantive and methodological specialties, I am a strong proponent for proactively preparing sociologists for leading and criticizing future convergence between social and computational sciences and deepened corporate participation in social science. Relevant training can be integrated into the existing intro-theory-stats-methods sequence at both undergraduate and graduate levels:
Sociological theory for and of big data Computational repertoire for all sociologists An interdisciplinary foundation in data management, digital privacy, open science, and corporate social science
title: Teaching
